title: Sitting on the Mall, Opening Question
summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the joint interview with Dorie Ann Ladner and Dr. Joyce Ann Ladner at the Library of Congress and frames an opening question about the ambivalence and complicated feeling they carried about the 1963 March on Washington.

title: Under Siege on the Front Line
summary: Dorie describes the ambivalence of coming to the March while the movement was under siege: Medgar Evers killed June 12, his funeral June 16, Lawrence Guyot beaten, Fannie Lou Hamer jailed and beaten in Winona, a Jackson store boycott, and arrests in Greenwood. The sisters knew they had to return to those towns.

title: Ruby Doris Sends Joyce to Harlem
summary: Joyce recounts learning of Medgar Evers's death by phone from Ruby Doris Smith Robinson while at a student conference in Lake Forest, Illinois. Ruby Doris persuaded her to go to New York to work under Bayard Rustin's direction at the Harlem March headquarters, where Joyce raised money, bringing back enough to run another bus each trip.

title: Bob Dylan and the Statler-Hilton
summary: Joyce remembers late New York nights with Bob Dylan strumming on the sofa and seeing Malcolm X speak on 125th Street, then traveling to Washington to stay at the Statler-Hilton, the March hotel. Early the next morning the group picketed the Justice Department over Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen, jailed for sedition in Americus, Georgia.

title: Lena Horne Yields the Interview
summary: On the Lincoln Memorial platform, Lena Horne declined a television interview and led nineteen-year-old Joyce by the hand up a scaffold to Nancy Dickerson, telling her to interview the young woman who lived in the South. Joyce recalls Josephine Baker, Marlon Brando, and her mother later reporting that the neighbors in Hattiesburg had seen her on TV.

title: Look White Men in the Eye
summary: Joyce recalls her mother teaching them at four and five to look white men in the eye and never look down, and the family mantra that they were as good as anybody. She then describes the on-platform crisis over John Lewis's speech, with James Forman and Courtland Cox, and SNCC's anger that an authority figure presumed to change their words.

title: De Lawd and Breaking an Area
summary: The sisters explain their ambivalent relationship with Martin Luther King, whom SNCC called De Lawd, contrasting his arrival at the crisis stage with SNCC's work of breaking an area: staying under the toughest conditions until a local community let them in. They left the day after the March, without lasting euphoria.

title: Bob Moses Drives to Birmingham
summary: Three weeks after the March, Bob Moses drove a school bus of SNCC workers to Birmingham for the funeral of the four girls killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing. Joyce reflects that the killing of children struck deeply, raising the fear that if they could do that to a child they could kill her brothers.

title: Stomach Illness and the Holiday Inn
summary: Dorie describes the stress-induced stomach illness she developed in Natchez, going to bed not expecting to wake up, with neighbors leaving phones off the hook for safety. Frustrated trying to bond out arrested demonstrators, she took a cab to the segregated Holiday Inn and paid the cashier to summon the chief of police.

title: Rage at the Masonic Temple
summary: The day after Medgar Evers was killed, Dorie ran up to two white police officers on motorcycles outside the Masonic Temple, screaming at them in a rage she cannot fully describe. They sat with heads bowed and never responded, and only late in life has she come to think they practiced non-violence with her.

title: The Beckwith Trials and a Volkswagen Escape
summary: Dorie attended both Beckwith trials, watched Ross Barnett shake the defendant's hand, and saw the Klan give Beckwith a standing ovation. She recounts district attorney William Waller's openly racist jury selection, being ejected by the judge, and escaping a mob with Dave Dennis and Mattie Bivens in a Volkswagen bus offered by a passerby.

title: Guilt, Courage, and a Black Scarf
summary: Joyce describes the guilt she carried in graduate school while Dorie stayed in dangerous Natchez, and her conviction that courage was acting in spite of fear. She recalls covering her white roommate Joan Trumpauer's blonde hair with a black scarf and putting her on the floor on the unlit Hanging Moss Road to Tougaloo.

title: The House and Mr. McCarthy's Still
summary: Asked to describe their home, the sisters detail the three-bedroom Palmers Crossing house with its front porch and the fenceless compound shared with grandparents and great aunt and uncle. They recount Mr. Joe McCarthy's bootleg whiskey still and the Friday-evening Mississippi National Guard raid that broke crates of liquor all night.
related_events: Mississippi National Guard raid on bootleg operation

title: Camp Shelby, the Crossing, and Miss Bama
summary: The sisters explain how their community served as a recreational outpost for Black soldiers from Camp Shelby, with the Inkwell Club and Club Desire drawing Chitlin' Circuit entertainers. They describe Palmers Crossing itself, the City of New Orleans train that brought the Chicago Defender, the Hudson grocery that became Hudson's salvage stores, and Miss Bama's house.

title: Mother Met Them on the Porch
summary: The sisters describe how their mother, divorced and for a time a single parent, told all her girls to get an education because no one could take it from them. They watched her meet insurance collectors on the front porch and never let them inside or treat her as inferior, modeling a no-nonsense pride.

title: Grandpa Ben Said We're As Good As Anybody
summary: The sisters trace their resistance ethos to a Creek Indian woman named Mary and a white man, Ben Willard of Rockingham, North Carolina, who would not enslave his own children. The mantra that they were as good as anybody passed through the generations. Their entrepreneur uncles refused to work for whites, illustrated by an uncle who walked away from a racist customer's broken TV.

title: Coining the Emmett Till Generation
summary: Joyce describes coining the term Emmett Till generation for post-war Black Southern youth whose parents, aunts, and uncles told them they would change things. She recalls Uncle Archie, a World War I veteran who served in France, telling them their generation would change things, and the Baptist liturgy they translated into the movement's songs and mass meetings.

title: Dr. McCloud and the Censored World
summary: The sisters recall the herbalist they called Dr. McCloud, a Hattiesburg NAACP member who brought them the Pittsburgh Courier, Chicago Defender, Jet, and Ebony each week. In a censored Mississippi with one TV station that called Black people slurs on screen, this outside reading material fueled their curiosity and showed them how others lived.

title: Meeting Medgar and the Kennard Frame-Up
summary: The sisters met Medgar Evers in high school when he came to set up the Hattiesburg NAACP youth chapter, with C.E. Clack and Vernon Dahmer as the motivating advisors and Dahmer's sister Hallean Beard, the church pianist, as Dorie's best friend and chaperone. They recall watching the frame-up of Clyde Kennard, jailed on a moonshine charge and held over a year.

title: Powerless Teenagers Clipping the News
summary: Looking back as fourteen and fifteen year olds who knew things were wrong but felt powerless, the sisters describe running to Hudson's for the Hattiesburg American at 4:15 each day during the Emmett Till case. Joyce clipped the articles; Dorie, wanting to be a lawyer, went to her teacher Mr. Clark and then memorized the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments for her own empowerment.

title: Why the Elders Followed Teenagers
summary: The sisters marvel that older people followed nineteen-year-olds, crediting it to taking themselves seriously and carrying a message the community had waited to hear. They recall getting waitresses and sharecroppers to leave their jobs and risk their lives at the Indianola courthouse where Fannie Lou Hamer registered in August 1962, and the Jet photograph of Emmett Till as the clarion call for their generation.
related_events: Fannie Lou Hamer voter registration August 1962

title: Mack Charles Parker and the Racism Unleashed
summary: Joyce recalls the second lynching they experienced, that of Mack Charles Parker in Poplarville, fifteen minutes from Hattiesburg, when international media came to the city and her mother pulled the boys close before dark. She reflects that such killings emboldened local racists to talk openly, and that the perpetrators lived in the community where everyone knew them.

title: Founding COFO and the Night Drive
summary: Dorie recounts the August 1962 founding of COFO in Clarksdale, her first time in the Delta. After a curfew violation, Dave Dennis was nearly jailed and the group drove in fear through the night, told that one of Emmett Till's killers was a night watchman in Ruleville, searching for Amzie Moore's house in Cleveland. The terror pushed her to commit to Delta organizing.

title: One Foot in College, Dorie Leaves Home
summary: Joyce explains the difference between them: she kept one foot in college while Dorie dropped out, saying she could not stay in school while people suffered. Dorie recounts leaving home with a small orange suitcase, telling her mother on the way out the door that she was going to Jackson to work with Bob Moses for her freedom, and arriving at the Freedom House on Rose Street.

title: Jackson State and the Tougaloo Nine
summary: The sisters recall James Meredith as a clear-headed strategist at Jackson State before he enrolled at Ole Miss, and demonstrating on behalf of the Tougaloo Nine, where they were tear-gassed. They describe crossing the street every Wednesday to talk with Medgar Evers about their freedom, and his bringing Dorie money sent by their mother.
related_events: James Meredith Ole Miss enrollment September 1962

title: The Prayer That Got Them Expelled
summary: Advised by Medgar Evers to act on campus, Dorie as dorm-council president called on Joyce to pray, and Joyce spoke of dark days ahead. The dorm matron reported the prayer to Dean Oscar Rogers, the sisters grew combative defending it, and after a march for the Tougaloo students and an early spring break, they and student-government president Walter Williams were expelled.

title: The Freedom House and the Freedom Riders
summary: Joyce describes the Jackson Freedom House, initially rented by the Freedom Riders, where Lawrence Guyot and Cole Ladell cooked for the group. She recalls first meeting the Freedom Riders upstairs at the Masonic Temple by Medgar's office, Tom Hayden, Charles McDew, Marion Barry, John Lewis, and Dion Diamond, and feeling she had been looking for them since she was fourteen.

title: Judge Cox and the Successor
summary: Dorie recalls being ordered out of a Freedom Rider trial by the judge, and the sisters describe US District Judge William Harold Cox referring to them as slurs in the courtroom. At the 50th Freedom Rider reunion, Joyce met the Black man who had replaced Cox on the bench, a remarkable turn fifty years later.

title: Cousin Victoria Gray, Teacher and Confidant
summary: The sisters describe their cousin Victoria Gray, beautiful and a model of going away to college at Wilberforce, who lived in Germany and returned exotic. She became active first with SCLC's Citizenship Education Program and then SNCC, working in Hattiesburg with Guyot and Frank Smith. They remember her as a teacher and confidant whose death still pains them.

title: Building the MFDP With Ella Baker
summary: The sisters explain the two levels of movement projects, strategic voter registration and events pushed to the national stage, and the building of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party through freedom ballots, precinct, district, and state conventions. They credit Ella Baker as the leader who taught them to listen and guided them to consensus with an unseen hand.

title: The Sojourner Fleet to Atlantic City
summary: Joyce recounts driving to Atlantic City in the Sojourner Fleet of cars, named by Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, stopping at Cleve Sellers's parents' home in Denmark, South Carolina. She describes the burned-out station wagon from the Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman murders displayed on the boardwalk and the movement's tactile way of showing people what segregation was like.

title: The WATS Line Goes Silent
summary: Dorie had seen Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman leaving Miami University in Oxford, Ohio to return to Mississippi after the church burning. Joyce describes the moment word came that they had not checked in on the WATS line and the staff knew they were dead. The sisters blame the federal government for not intervening and reflect on the movement's walking wounded.

title: The Natchez Bombing and Little Richard
summary: Dorie recounts a Klan attempt to bomb the Natchez SNCC office that hit the wrong house, an integrated cafe belonging to Jake Fishman, and how Mr. Archie Curtis the funeral director escorted her through the mob to retrieve the voter-registration cards. She describes quieting a harassing late-night caller by promising her a Little Richard concert ticket.

title: Greenwood, Police Dogs, and John Doar
summary: Dorie recalls working all day in the Greenwood SNCC office, learning it was bombed that night, and cleaning up the files the next morning before going right back out. At the Leflore County Courthouse, police dogs chased the group and a young Marian Wright, who had interned at the Justice Department, called John Doar from a pay phone.

title: Terrorism, the Back Seat, and PTSD
summary: Dorie reflects that the word terrorism does not faze her because she lived through churches and homes bombed beyond counting. Unable to drive, she always sat in the back seat so another could take the wheel if the driver was shot, recalling Jimmy Travis shot in 1963. She describes how some memories flash across her mind like PTSD, and how Joyce sublimated trauma into driven work.

title: Dahmer's Death and Mrs. Turnbow's Pistol
summary: Joyce, who later worked in a Level 1 trauma unit, names her worst movement moment: the 6 a.m. call from her mother in January 1966 that Vernon Dahmer was killed, after years of repression broke through. She recalls lobbying the Oregon delegation with Representative Edith Green and Senator Wayne Morse alongside Mrs. Hartman Turnbow, who carried a pistol in a brown paper bag.

title: Genealogy After the Records Opened
summary: Dorie describes her genealogy quest after Mississippi opened its slave records to Black researchers in 1992, drawn by her mother's family being visibly different, malato and Indian. Through cross-referenced research, others studying the same great-great-grandfather connected her to white Willard descendants in Georgia, Alabama, and London, leading to reunions in Mobile, Alabama.
related_events: Mississippi archives opened to Black researchers 1992

title: The Willard and Mary Line
summary: Dorie lays out the traced maternal line: Benjamin F. Willard, born 1805 in Rockingham, North Carolina, and Mary, a Black Creek Indian also born 1805, who came together to the Alabama-Mississippi territory and had thirteen children. The line runs through Frank (1825), Jerry Willard (about 1870), Joseph Collins Willard and Martha Gates, to their mother, born May 14, 1921 and died January 23, 1995.

title: The White Side Resists
summary: The sisters reflect that they were not surprised by the white side of the family, though those relatives were. They describe the Willard descendants who deny the connection and the large Creole Favre family in Louisiana, which includes Brett Favre and Jimmy Buffett and holds integrated reunions, who decided early to stay in the white race rather than the Black race.

title: Teach the History, and Closing
summary: Asked what part of the civil rights narrative most needs attention, Joyce answers that the schools must teach the history so that elementary, high school, and college students know what happened, countering revisionists. The interview closes with thanks and the Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.
